summary:
The primary mechanism involves resorption at the back part of the chin bone allowing more room for wisdom teeth. Crowded front teeth do not directly cause this process since they are compensating due to overall insufficient space throughout the mouth instead.
To summarize Agent1’s reasoning into two concise sentences focusing only on key clinical points related to your query about creating arch space for second and third molars' eruption:

Resorption primarily occurs at the posterior region of the mandibular body to create necessary space for these molars to erupt properly. Anterior tooth crowding does not directly lead to increased space availability behind; such overcrowding reflects an issue across multiple areas within the dental arcade necessitating compensation through lateral or distal movement/eruption pathways including those provided by resorptive changes near the angle of the mandible. 

This summary captures essential aspects without extraneous details while addressing core mechanisms involved according to presented information regarding arch space development specifically pertinent to questions around second/third molar emergence dynamics.
